facts_disclosed: Joon-seo wedding Sept 20, bride Soo-yeon pharmacist

turns.content: I have some news that I am still absorbing. My son Joon-seo called last night to tell me he is getting married. The date is September 20. I think I should be entirely happy about this, and I am — but there is a complexity to the feeling that I did not anticipate.

Congratulations — that's wonderful news. And I think the complexity you're feeling is completely natural. A parent's joy at their child's wedding is rarely simple, especially when the family picture has changed.

Tell me about the complexity. What's making this feel layered for you?

Her name is Soo-yeon. She is a pharmacist — a serious, intelligent woman. Joon-seo brought her to Busan twice last year, and I could see how careful she was with him. She asks questions and listens to the answers. Eunji would have loved her, I think. She would have seen in Soo-yeon the same qualities she valued — steadiness, warmth, attention to detail. And that is precisely where the complexity lies. Eunji is not here to meet her. She will not be at the wedding.

That's a profound kind of bittersweet — knowing exactly how much your wife would have loved this woman, and holding that knowledge alone. The joy of your son finding a good partner is real, but it arrives in a space where someone essential is missing.

The fact that you can see Eunji in Soo-yeon's qualities — that she asks questions and listens — says something about the continuity in your family. Your son chose someone his mother would have recognized.

September 20 — that's about five and a half months away. Has the planning begun?

They are handling most of it from Seoul. The ceremony will be at a wedding hall there — Soo-yeon's family is also in the Seoul area, so it is the practical choice. I have told Joon-seo that I will help with whatever is needed, but I think the honest truth is that this is their event to shape. My role is to be present and to give the father's speech. And the speech is the thing I am most anxious about — not because of public speaking, but because I will be standing where Eunji should be standing beside me.

That's a generous perspective — letting them shape the day while you focus on being fully present. And I understand the anxiety about the speech. Standing at the front alone, carrying both roles — that makes the private grief public in a way your daily life doesn't.

But the empty space beside you isn't a failure. It's a testament. Everyone in that room will understand what it means — that you raised your son through loss, and that Eunji's absence is felt because her presence mattered so deeply.

Have you and Joon-seo discussed how to handle the ceremony in that regard?

He mentioned that Soo-yeon suggested they could modify the ceremony in a way that acknowledges his mother without making it somber. She is thoughtful in that way. I do not know what form that would take, but I trust her instinct. A pharmacist understands dosage — she will know how much remembrance is the right amount without overwhelming the occasion.

Dosage of remembrance — that's a beautiful way to frame it. And it speaks well of Soo-yeon that she raised it herself rather than waiting for someone else to. Some couples might avoid the topic entirely, which usually makes the absence louder, not quieter.

Small gestures often work best: a framed photo at the parents' table, a brief mention woven into the ceremony. These things acknowledge reality without changing the tone of the day. Have you started thinking about the speech itself?

Not yet. The words must come from me — I do not want a template. But I think the hardest part will be speaking about Eunji without making the speech about grief. It should be about Joon-seo and Soo-yeon, about their future. Yet Eunji is part of Joon-seo's story, and to omit her would be dishonest. The boundary between honoring her memory and indulging my own sadness is a narrow one. I will have to walk it carefully.

You've already identified the exact challenge, which means you'll navigate it well. The key is making Eunji's mention serve the couple's story — not "I miss your mother" but something closer to "your mother shaped the man Soo-yeon is marrying." That keeps the focus forward while acknowledging the past honestly.

You're someone who has spent a lifetime working with precision — at the board and in the lecture hall. If anyone can walk that narrow line with grace, it's you.

That is a helpful reframing. The mention of Eunji should be in service of the couple, not in service of my grief. I will hold onto that principle when I begin writing. For now, I think I will sit with the news a while longer. My son is getting married. It is a good thing. I think I will pour myself some barley tea and allow myself to simply be happy about it, even if the happiness has a shadow. Good evening.

Happiness with a shadow is still happiness — and a shadow only exists where there's light. Enjoy the barley tea and the good news. September will come when it's ready, and so will the right words.

Good evening. Congratulations again, to you and to the whole family.
